html:
XIX

With her materials and patterns laid out on the dining-room table, Mattie Farnham was trying to cut out a dress for her Margaret, an undertaking which was going jerkily because of the arrival, seriatim, of the children from school. They came in at different times, as suited their different ages and their rank in the hierarchy of grades. Little Jim in the first grade was free at two, Loren in the fourth was turned loose at three, and Margaret and Ellen appeared soon after four. The hour of the arrival varied, but the manner was identical: a clatter of hurried feet on the porch, the bursting open of the door, and the questing yell of вАЬMother! MoвАСoвАСother!вАЭ

Mattie always answered with an вАЬOo-hoo!вАЭ on two notes, adding, вАЬin the diвАСiвАСining-room!вАЭ but she never waited for them to come to find her. She always laid down her work and all thought of it and hastened to give the returned wanderer a hug and kiss and run an anxious eye over his aspect to see what had happened to him during his day out in the world.

вАЬJimmy, you look tired. Did you eat your lunch good? Come on with me and get a piece of bread and butter.вАЭ

вАЬSay, Mother, Teacher picked me out to say the good morning greeting to the whole school this morning at Assembly.вАЭ

вАЬDid she? Which one did you say? WerenвАЩt you scared? Say it to me. LetвАЩs hear.вАЭ

A half hour later they would still be in the pantry, Jimmy swinging his legs from his motherвАЩs cushiony lap, telling her between mouthfuls about everything that had happened in the long interval since he had seen her last. Mattie listened eagerly, stroking the hair back from the square white forehead, gloating greedily over the changing expressions on the little open, rosy face.

Then Jimmy wanted to know what she was doing and trotted back with her to the dining-room and had to have the nature of patterns explained to him, and hung over her as she worked, rumpling up the paper and getting in her way, his tongue and hers flying together.

Somehow it was time for Loren. Wherever had that last hour gone to? The crash of the opening door, the shrill whoop of вАЬMother! MoвАСoвАСother!вАЭ And it all began again, this time with an exciting account of how Teacher gave Morton Cummings the awfulest calling-down you ever heard for copying off of Sadie BennettвАЩs paper. Both Jimmy and Mother were spellbound.

But MargaretвАЩs dress did not progress very rapidly. At a quarter of four Mattie still had the sleeves to cut out, and sheвАЩd have to put her mind on them because she hadnвАЩt bought enough dimity and they would have to be pieced under the arm. вАЬLoren, you and Jimmy run out and play a while, wonвАЩt you, thatвАЩs good boys. MotherвАЩs got to get this done.вАЭ

вАЬMercy! Evangeline Knapp would have had that dress all cut out and basted up.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

And then, right out of a clear sky, came the unheralded thunderbolt of a new ideaвБ†вАФhow could it have come like that! She had not thought of the Knapps, not once all that day. She had been wrapped up in her work and in the children; yet the minute she had thought of EvaвАЩs nameвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ it was all there, as though she had been studying over them for weeks! Everything in her head had shucked together different, like when you look in a kaleidoscope and give it a shake, and thereвАЩs a new design. Why! Why! One thing after another came to herвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ how could it be she had never thought of it before? It was so plain nowвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ why, yes!

The inquiring shout of Margaret and Ellen had no response. Surprised and aggrieved, they pushed on hastily in search of their mother and found her dropped into a chair by the dining-room table, her big scissors in her hand, her eyes wide and fixed. She answered them absently, she hardly looked at them, she never noticed that Ellen had lost her hair-ribbon, she interrupted MargaretвАЩs account of how Maria ElwellвАЩs petticoat had come off by jumping up and saying suddenly as though she didnвАЩt even know that Margaret was talking, вАЬSee here, girls, IвАЩve got to run over to your Uncle LesterвАЩs for something. You keep an eye on Jimmy, will you?вАЭ

What was the matter with Mother anyhow, Margaret and Ellen asked themselves over their four oвАЩclock pieces of gingerbread. But they were not much worried. There was never very much the matter with Mother.

She hurried so that she was puffing as she went up the porch steps of the Knapp houseвБ†вАФand yet when she opened the door she did not know why she had come nor what to say. Henry and Helen were just in also, enjoying cookies and milk and telling their father about the events of the day. The sight of the cookies gave Mattie her cue.

вАЬDo those spice-cookies agree with Henry?вАЭ she asked.

вАЬSure they do,вАЭ said Lester. вАЬEverything does nowadays! Henry seems to have grown right out of that weak stomach of his. He eats like a wolf, I tell him. The doctor says they do sometimes outgrow those childish things as they get near their teens.вАЭ

вАЬOh, yes, as they get near their teens,вАЭ said Mattie.

A moment later she asked, вАЬHelen, arenвАЩt you fatter than you used to be? Seems as though you were lots fuller in the face.вАЭ

вАЬDid you just get around to notice that, Aunt Mattie?вАЭ said Helen, laughing. вАЬYou ought to see me trying to get into a last summerвАЩs dress. They donвАЩt come togetherвБ†вАФmy!вБ†вАФthereвАЩs that much of a gap.вАЭ She showed with her hands how wide a gap it was.

вАЬHelen has put on eight pounds,вАЭ explained Lester. вАЬThe school nurse says all the children are gaining like everything, now they serve milk at recess-time.вАЭ

вАЬOh, yes, milk at recess-time,вАЭ said Aunt Mattie.

Helen and Henry finished their cookies and tore out to inspect their poultry. The children and Lester had gone into the chicken-business on a small scale and were raising some brooder chicks in a packing-case chicken-house in the back yard. Stephen was there already, hanging over the low wire-netting вАЬwatching their tail-feathers grow,вАЭ as he said.

Lester quoted this as he wheeled himself to the open door where Mattie stood looking out at the children fussing maternally over the little peeping yellow balls. вАЬHonest to goodness, Mattie, their tail and wing feathers do come in so fast you can see them grow.вАЭ He added, вАЬIвАЩm watching feathers grow, too. Stephen is fairly sprouting wings heвАЩs so good! ItвАЩs because he can play out of doors again, I suppose, after the winter. WeвАЩve had such lovely weather of late.вАЭ

вАЬYes, it must be because he can play out of doors again,вАЭ said Aunt Mattie.

As they turned back into the kitchen, where a batch of bread was ready to be put into the oven, she asked, вАЬLester, arenвАЩt you better of your indigestion lately?вАЭ

вАЬSh!вАЭ he warned her whimsically, his finger at his lips. вАЬDonвАЩt mention it aloud. I havenвАЩt had any in months. But I donвАЩt want it spoken about. Leave sleeping dogs lie. The doctor always said it was nervous, you know. I donвАЩt know much about the geography of my innards, but IвАЩve thought once or twice that maybe that awful shakeup my nervous system got might have sorted things over into the right pile, as far as digestion goes. ItвАЩs not, however,вАЭ he said with a sudden grim, black look at his paralyzed legs, вАЬa cure for indigestion that I could recommend.вАЭ

The tears sprang into MattieвАЩs eyes as she turned her face away, вАЬItвАЩs pretty hard!вАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt pretend itвАЩs any picnic. But itвАЩs of no consequence of course.вАЭ He was able to say this with a bare and utter sincerity.

вАЬLook here, Lester!вАЭ she broke out. вАЬWhy couldnвАЩt youвБ†вАФI donвАЩt believe but what you could go and be a professor somewhere in a University or a High School. Professors donвАЩt have to walk around. And youвАЩve always set such store by poetry and books and everything. There canвАЩt be anybody whoвАЩs more.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

Lester broke in with a laugh at her absurdity. вАЬWhy, you dear old girl, you donвАЩt know what youвАЩre talking about. IвАЩd make a mess of what they want in a school just as much as at the store. What makes you think colleges want teachers who love literature? They want somebody who can make young people sit still and listen whether they feel like it or not. They want somebody who can вАШkeep orderвАЩ in a class room and drill students on dates so they can pass examinations. I couldnвАЩt do that! And IвАЩd loathe forcing literature down the throats of boys and girls who didnвАЩt want it as IвАЩd loathe selling things to people who didnвАЩt need them. IвАЩd be just a dead loss at it the way I always am.вАЭ

Seeing that she did not follow this, he added concretely, вАЬBesides I could no more get a job without all the right certificates than I could set up shop as a doctor. Nowadays colleges want you to be a Ph.вАКD. And there isnвАЩt a crossroads High School thatвАЩd look at a man who had only had three years in a State University fifteen years ago and had been making a failure of keeping accounts in a department store ever since.вАЭ

Mattie recognized the irrefutable nature of all this. вАЬYes, I see,вАЭ she said sadly.

вАЬIsnвАЩt Mattie the ignorant, impractical old infant!вАЭ thought Lester.

She got up now, with a long breath, and silently took herself off.

Although it was long past time to start supper, she did not go home. She went straight down to WillingвАЩs and into the Cloak-and-Suits. Eva was busy with customers as usual. вАЬEverybody wants Eva to wait on them,вАЭ thought Mattie, sitting down heavily. Her eyes were fixed on Evangeline. What a splendid woman she was, and, now she had some money to spend on her clothes, what a stylish-looking woman! There wasnвАЩt anybody in town could hold a candle to her. Mattie made these reflections automatically. These were always the first thoughts which came to her when she saw Eva.

But today, ravaged as she was by this new perception, in which she was so all alone, her mind dwelt little on style. What she saw today was EvaвАЩs face, alert, interested, sympathetic, and EvaвАЩs eyes, which had always had, so Mattie remembered, вАЬa sort of wild look,вАЭ now so shining and quiet, looking from the suits she was showing to her customers. They were a couple of women from out in the country, elderly mother and grown-up daughter. Mattie was too far off to hear what was said, but she understood perfectly from the pantomime and from the expression of the three faces, what the situation was. The two women had thrown themselves on EvaвАЩs taste to help them make up their minds, and Eva, looking at them intently, was putting herself wholeheartedly in their places so that she could give them her best judgment. How happy she looked!

As she watched, a lump came into MattieвАЩs throat, and she felt her eyes hot and misty. What in time was the matter with her? She swallowed hard and looked away and tried to think of something else. But she could not. Lester and Stephen and Henry and HelenвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and Eva!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ came and stood before her eyesвБ†вАФher opened eyes.

вАЬMy goodness! I mustnвАЩt get to crying here in the store!вАЭ she thought, alarmed, starting up and going to the window.

When she turned around, EvaвАЩs customers had made their decision, a momentous one, judging from the relief on their faces. The three women were chatting and smiling together, relaxed and cheerful. Mattie heard Eva say, вАЬI know youвАЩll take the greatest comfort in it!вАЭ She went with her customers to the head of the stairs, talking like an old friend. They shook hands with her, respectfully, cordially. Then she turned around and came almost running back towards Mattie. вАЬMattie, IвАЩve got something to tell you,вАЭ she said hurriedly, smiling. She looked around her to make sure no one was near and lowered her voice, вАЬMiss FlynnвАЩs niece has died and leaves four little children and their father wants Miss FlynnвБ†вАФhe hasnвАЩt got any relative of his ownвБ†вАФto go and bring the children up and keep house for him. HeвАЩs in the greenhouse business at Cleveland. Plenty of money. And she is going.вАЭ

Mattie did not understand this. She understood few things at once. She saw nothing but EvaвАЩs curving, smiling lips and bright shining eyes. She understood them with no difficulty.

вАЬDonвАЩt you see?вАЭ whispered Eva. вАЬSomebodyвАЩs going to be moved up to her place, head of the department. TheyвАЩre going to give me a try at it. ArenвАЩt they good! Mattie! ItвАЩs three thousand a year! And a bonus for extra sales! And such fascinating work! IвАЩm wild to get my hands on it and see what I can do with the salesgirls. Oh, Mattie, we can begin to lay by a little something every month for the childrenвАЩs college. Perhaps we can buy a Ford that Lester can get out in with the children. Oh, Mattie!вАЭ

At this Mattie disgraced herself and showed once more, as she said apologetically, what an idiot she was by bursting into senseless, hysteric tears and having to be carried off in haste to the toilet-room to cold water and smelling salts.

вАЬIвАЩve felt all squimbly this whole afternoon,вАЭ she explained, blowing her nose. вАЬI donвАЩt know whatвАЩs the matter with me. Old fool, I guess.вАЭ

вАЬWell, it almost makes me feel like crying myself,вАЭ said Eva, holding out a glass of water to her. вАЬItвАЩs come so soon, so much sooner than I dared to hope. And it will mean so much to Lester and the children. TheyвАЩd never have had a college education any other way. Why, Mattie, IвАЩve kept thinking all day about the hymn, вАШGod moves in a mysterious way, His.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЩвАКвАЭ

вАЬDonвАЩt!вАЭ said Mattie huskily. вАЬYouвАЩll get me started again.вАЭ

вАЬOf course,вАЭ Eva said now, вАЬitвАЩs dreadfully hard for a mother to be separated from her.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

Mattie broke in hastily, as if to change the subject, вАЬEva, how is that eczema of yours lately?вАЭ

Mrs.¬†Knapp rolled up a fashionably wide sleeve and showed a clean, white upper arm. вАЬDr.¬†Merritt finally found a cure,вАЭ she said, вАЬa new kind of ointment he heard about in a medical convention. ItвАЩs worked like a charm. I havenвАЩt had a touch of eczemaвБ†вАФwhy, in I donвАЩt know when! It took the doctor long enough to get around to it, but he finally did.вАЭ

It was half-past five when Mrs.¬†Farnham left the store, but still she did not start home. вАЬLet them wait for supper!вАЭ she thought, desperately. What was supper compared to some other things! She hurried heavily along towards Dr.¬†MerrittвАЩs house, hoping to goodness he would be in.

He was, sitting on the porch, reading the evening paper. вАЬHello, Mrs.¬†Farnham,вАЭ he said, surprised to see her. вАЬI didnвАЩt think IвАЩd ever get any business out of your family. WhoвАЩs broken a leg?вАЭ

вАЬWeвАЩre all right,вАЭ she told him. вАЬI wanted to ask you about the Knapps. You know IвАЩm sort of related to Mr.¬†Knapp. IвАЩve been wondering what you really thought about himвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ whether heвАЩll ever be cured, I mean.вАЭ

The doctor noticed that her voice trembled as she spoke. What a good-natured creature she was, taking other peopleвАЩs troubles so to heart.

He hesitated. It was not at all his habit to talk about his patients to outsiders, least of all to any such chatterbox as Mrs.¬†Farnham. But he had thought several times lately that, if Lester Knapp were to make any progress, he would need to start a campaign to dry up the gushing spring of family sympathy. He knew all about that sort of campaign from much experience, but he was never resigned to the necessity for it. вАЬDarn families and their sympathy!вАЭ he often said impatiently. вАЬThey вАШpoor-CharlieвАЩ and вАШpoor-MaryвАЩ more sick people into their graves than we doctors do.вАЭ

He had long suspected that well-meaning Mrs.¬†Farnham did a good deal of вАЬpoor-LesteringвАЭ at the Knapps. Maybe this was a chance to head her off, to get her mind started along a new track. Of course he must remember to use the simplest, most elementary language with her. She was really almost an illiterate.

вАЬIвАЩll tell you, Mrs.¬†Farnham, just what I think about the case. As near as I can make out, the effusion of blood within the spinal canal has been safely absorbed, or nearly so. There seems to be no displacement or injury to the spinal bones; there is no wasting away of the muscles as would be the case if the spinal cord were injured. There is, I believe, good reason to hope that the loss of power in his legs is a sequel of organic conditions which have now passed away. The case now needs a psychic treatment rather than a mechanical.вАЭ

вАЬOrganic?вАЭ said Mrs.¬†Farnham, faintly. The word made her think of church.

вАЬI mean that in my opinion no physical lesion now exists in spite of the abnormal sensations which Mr.¬†Knapp still feels. We must try toning up the general health, overcoming the shock to the nervous system. As soon as the weather permits, I shall try heliotherapy.вАЭ

Mrs.¬†Farnham caught her breath.

вАЬThat is, treatment of the affected areas by direct exposure to sunlight. They have done wonderful things in France with that treatment in just this sort of trouble. And of course at any time any sort of sudden nervous stimulus might do the business. You see, Mrs.¬†Farnham, Mr.¬†KnappвАЩs case is now like that of the people who are cured at Lourdes, or by Cou√©. The very same sort of phenomenon.вАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt understand very well,вАЭ said Mattie humbly. вАЬWhat I wanted to know wasвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ her voice faltered, вАЬdo you think you can cure him?вАЭ

вАЬIsnвАЩt she the dumbbell!вАЭ thought the doctor.

He went on aloud, hoping she would repeat his words to Mrs.¬†Knapp, вАЬDonвАЩt you say anything about it, Mrs.¬†Farnham, especially to Mrs.¬†Knapp. I donвАЩt want to crow till we are out of the woods. I wouldnвАЩt say anything to you if you were not a relative and a sensible woman. I donвАЩt want them to have a breath of it, for fear of disappointment.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ (How strangely she was looking at him, her face so white and anxious!) He brought it out roundly, вАЬYes, Mrs.¬†Farnham, just between us, I really believe I can cure him.вАЭ

She gave a low cry that was like a wail. вАЬOh, Doctor!вАЭ she cried, appalled, staring at him.

What was the matter with the woman, now? He stared back at her, blankly, startled, entirely at a loss.

Another look came into her eyes, an imploring, imploring look. She clasped her hands beseechingly. вАЬOh, Doctor!вАЭ she begged him, in a quavering voice.

From her eyes, from her voice, from her beseeching attitude, from her trembling hands, he took in her meaningвБ†вАФtook it in with a tingling shock of surprise at first. And then with a deep recognition of it as something he had known all along.

She saw the expression change in his face, saw the blank look go out of his eyes, saw the understanding look come in.

It was a long rich interchange of meanings that took place as they sat staring hard at each other, the gaunt, middle-aged man no longer merely a doctor, the dull middle-aged woman, transfigured to essential wisdom by the divination of her loving heart. Profound and human things passed from one to the other.

Mattie heard someone stirring in the house. вАЬI must go! I must go!вАЭ she said groaningly. She limped down the path. Her feet were aching like the toothache with the haste of her expeditions that afternoon.

Half an hour later they had to come out and call the doctor to supper, fairly to shout in his ear he was so sunk in his thoughts, the evening paper lying unread across his knees.

вАЬMercy me! DidnвАЩt you hear the supper bell?вАЭ cried his wife. вАЬItвАЩs been ringing like anything!вАЭ
